title: Best Online Glasses in Canada: Where to Buy Eyewear in 2026
description: Compare the best places to buy glasses online in Canada. We cover pricing, lens quality, insurance coverage, and when visiting a local optician is worth it.
tldr: Online retailers like Clearly and Bonlook offer budget frames from $30-$150, but you trade away precise fitting, in-person adjustments, and real-time insurance billing. For progressives and complex prescriptions, an in-store experience still wins.

definition.text: Online glasses retailers are e-commerce platforms that sell prescription eyewear directly to consumers, allowing customers to upload their prescription and choose frames without visiting a physical store.

sections.h2: How Online Glasses Shopping Works in Canada
sections.content:
Ordering glasses online follows a fairly standard process: you enter your prescription details (sphere, cylinder, axis, PD), pick a frame, choose your lens type, and wait for delivery. Most Canadian retailers ship within 7–14 business days.

The appeal is obvious — lower overhead means lower prices. But there are trade-offs. You can't try frames on your actual face (virtual try-on tools are improving but still imperfect), and nobody's there to check your pupillary distance or adjust the fit when they arrive.

For simple single-vision prescriptions with mild corrections, online ordering works reasonably well. For progressive lenses, high prescriptions, or anyone who needs same-day glasses, an in-person visit saves a lot of headaches.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

sections.h2: Top Online Glasses Retailers in Canada (2026)
sections.content:
Here's a look at the most popular options available to Canadian shoppers:

Clearly

Canada's largest online eyewear retailer, owned by EssilorLuxottica. They carry house brands and some designer names, with frames starting around $30. Free shipping on orders over $50. They have a few showroom locations in Vancouver and Toronto, but nothing in Alberta.

Bonlook

Quebec-based brand with stylish, Canadian-designed frames in the $150–$250 range. Good lens options including blue light filtering and progressives. Physical stores mostly in Quebec and Ontario.

Warby Parker

American company that now ships to Canada. Known for the home try-on program (they mail you 5 frames to test). Prices start at $120 USD, and you'll pay in US dollars plus potential duty.

Zenni Optical

The budget king — frames from $7 USD. Quality varies widely, and returns can be complicated since they ship from the US. No Canadian customer service line.

Kits

Vancouver-based, offers a first-pair-free promotion regularly. Decent quality for the price point, with faster Canadian shipping than US-based competitors.

sections.h2: Online vs. In-Store: An Honest Comparison
sections.content:
FactorOnlineIn-Store
Price (single vision)$30–$200$150–$400+
Price (progressives)$100–$350$300–$600+
Try before buyingVirtual only (or mail try-on)Yes, full selection
Fitting & adjustmentsDIYProfessional, included
Insurance direct billingRarelyUsually yes
Turnaround time7–14 daysSame day to 1 week
Complex Rx accuracyHigher error rateVerified on-site
Returns30 days typical, shipping hassleImmediate exchange

The price gap narrows considerably once you factor in insurance. Most employer plans cover $200–$400 for frames and lenses every two years, and stores like Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bill major Alberta insurers — meaning you might pay little to nothing out of pocket for quality glasses.

When comparing options, consider your daily routine and visual priorities rather than just price. The "best" choice is the one that matches how you actually use your eyes throughout the day. Someone who works on a computer for 8 hours has very different needs than someone who primarily drives or reads. An honest conversation with your optometrist about your daily visual demands will point you toward the right option far more effectively than online reviews.

Keep in mind that you're not locked into one choice forever. Many people own multiple pairs for different situations — dedicated computer glasses for the office, progressives for daily wear, and prescription sunglasses for driving. Charm Optical carries 500+ frames across every style and price point, making it easy to build a collection that covers all your visual needs. Their opticians can advise which lens types pair best with each frame style. Same-day glasses are available for many single vision prescriptions.

Online ordering is a solid choice when you have a simple, stable single-vision prescription (say, under ±4.00), you already know your frame size and style preferences, and you're buying a backup or fashion pair where perfect optics aren't critical.

It also works well if you live somewhere remote without a local optician — though most Canadian cities and larger towns have multiple options.

sections.h2: When You Should Buy In-Store Instead
sections.content:
Head to an optician if you need progressive or bifocal lenses (the fitting measurements are critical and very hard to get right at home), if your prescription is strong or has significant astigmatism, if you want designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, or Gucci, or if you want to use your insurance benefits with direct billing.

Children should always be fitted in person. Their faces are still growing, and an improper fit can cause discomfort or even affect how well the prescription works.

sections.h2: Tips for Getting It Right Online
sections.content:
If you do order online, a few things will improve your odds of a good outcome:

  • Get a current prescription — most provinces require prescriptions to be less than 2 years old. Book an eye exam if yours is expired.
  • Measure your PD accurately — pupillary distance is the most common source of online ordering errors. Have a friend help, or ask your optometrist to include it on your prescription.
  • Know your frame size — check the inside arm of a pair that fits you well. You'll see numbers like 52-18-140 (lens width, bridge, temple length).
  • Start simple — order single-vision lenses first. Save progressives and specialty lenses for an in-person fitting.
  • Check the return policy — some retailers charge restocking fees or don't accept returns on prescription lenses at all.

This is where in-store shopping has a clear advantage for most Canadians. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, Great-West Life, AISH, and Alberta Works all cover prescription eyewear — and local optical stores can bill these providers directly.

With online retailers, you typically pay the full amount upfront and submit a claim for reimbursement afterward. The process can take weeks, and some plans reimburse less for purchases made outside their preferred provider network.

If you're in the Edmonton area, it's worth checking your coverage before ordering online. You might be surprised how affordable in-store glasses become once insurance kicks in.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter — no upfront costs and no claim forms to submit afterwards.

Whether you're buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the entire process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Are online glasses as good quality as in-store?
faqs.a: For basic single-vision lenses, online quality is generally acceptable. However, premium lens coatings, progressive lenses, and frame durability tend to be better from established optical stores. The biggest difference is the fitting — poorly fitted glasses cause headaches and blurry zones regardless of lens quality.

faqs.q: Can I use my Alberta health insurance for online glasses?
faqs.a: Most Alberta insurance plans (Blue Cross, Canada Life, etc.) will reimburse online purchases, but you'll need to pay upfront and file a claim. In-store at places like Charm Optical, they can direct-bill your insurance so you only pay the difference.

faqs.q: How do I get my pupillary distance for ordering online?
faqs.a: Ask your optometrist to include PD on your prescription, or measure it at home using a ruler and mirror. For the most accurate measurement, have it done during an eye exam — many Edmonton optometrists include PD if you ask.

faqs.q: Is it safe to buy progressive lenses online?
faqs.a: It's possible but risky. Progressives require precise measurements for the corridor placement, and even small errors make them uncomfortable or unusable. Most opticians recommend getting progressives fitted in person.

faqs.q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
faqs.a: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.

faqs.q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
faqs.a: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.
